plainMeaning: On top of that pillar stands a huge jewelled wheel a full yojana around — this is the Wheel-of-Dharma holy place, a fine ship for crossing the sea of rebirth.
recitation: tassovari rayaṇāmayaṁ, joyaṇa parimaṇḍalaṁ pavara cakkaṁ; taṁ dhammacakka titthaṁ, bhavajala-nihi pavara bohitthaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: sandhi of tassa (gen. sg. of taṁ, 'of that') + uvari (Skt. upari, 'above'); refers back to the jewel staff/pillar (daṇḍa) of the preceding verse
wordByWord.notes: the locative-sense compound sets this verse directly atop the structure built in v.57

wordByWord.etymology: rayaṇa (Skt. ratna, 'jewel') + -maya ('made of'); neut. agreeing with cakkaṁ
Skt. yojana, an ancient unit of distance; here a measure of the wheel's span
wordByWord.notes: qualifies parimaṇḍalaṁ — the wheel is one yojana in girth

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. parimaṇḍala, from pari ('around') + maṇḍala ('circle, disc'); MW: 'circular, round, a circumference'

wordByWord.notes: qualifies cakkaṁ here; echoed again in line 2 (pavara bohitthaṁ)
the physical jewelled wheel, identified in line 2 as the dharma-cakra

wordByWord.etymology: demonstrative tad-, neut. nom. sg.; resumes cakkaṁ from line 1
dhamma (Skt. dharma) + cakka (cakra); tatpuruṣa, 'the wheel of dharma'
wordByWord.notes: names the tīrtha — the Dharma-cakra shrine (traditionally at Takṣaśilā)

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tīrtha, 'ford, crossing'; neut. nom. sg.

wordByWord.notes: forms a single image with the next word: bhava-jala-nihi

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. nidhi, 'treasure, receptacle'; jala-nidhi = 'water-store', i.e. the ocean
wordByWord.notes: bhava-jala-nihi = 'the ocean of worldly existence'; a variant of the preface's bhava-jaladhi

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pravara (see above); repeated for poetic chime, now qualifying bohitthaṁ
wordByWord.notes: the deliberate repetition links the 'supreme wheel' to the 'supreme ship'

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bohittha (Skt. pota), 'ship'; attested in Prakrit lexica as a means of crossing water
wordByWord.notes: neut. nom. sg.; the crossing-image central to the hymn

literalTranslation: Above that (pillar), jewel-wrought, a yojana in circumference, the supreme wheel; that Dharma-wheel is the tīrtha — the excellent ship upon the ocean of worldly existence.
idiomaticTranslation: Crowning that jewelled pillar rises a wheel of gems, a full yojana in its round, a wheel beyond compare. That very wheel is the Dharma-Cakra tīrtha — the matchless ship that ferries souls across the boundless ocean of birth and death.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse caps the Dharma-cakra tīrtha sequence by placing the crowning emblem atop the structure raised in the previous verse. There, on the spot hallowed by the Jina's feet, a jewel pedestal (rayaṇamaya pīḍha) and a yojana-tall jewel staff (daṇḍa) were set up; here, 'above that' (tassovari), a jewelled wheel a full yojana in circumference is mounted. The poet builds in two movements. Line 1 is pure description — material (rayaṇāmayaṁ), dimension (joyaṇa parimaṇḍalaṁ) and praise (pavara cakkaṁ) — letting the eye climb the monument. Line 2 then transforms description into identification: 'that' wheel (taṁ) is named the dhamma-cakka, declared a tīrtha, and finally re-imaged as a pavara bohittha, a 'supreme ship.' The repeated pavara is not padding: it deliberately yokes the 'supreme wheel' of line 1 to the 'supreme ship' of line 2, so the same object is simultaneously a turning wheel of teaching and a vessel of rescue. Note the swift metaphor-shift within a single half-line — a wheel that is also a boat — which works precisely because both are tīrtha-functions: one proclaims dharma, the other carries the worshipper across. The verse thus completes the Takṣaśilā episode (traditionally the spot where Ṛṣabha stood in kāyotsarga and where the Dharma-cakra was raised in commemoration) and hands the narrative on to the next site, Ahicchatra, where Pārśva is sheltered by Dharaṇendra.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The dharma-cakra is the standing emblem of a Tīrthaṅkara's preaching — the 'turning' of the wheel of dharma at the samavasaraṇa — so to call a jewelled wheel a tīrtha is to say that the monument participates in that very act of teaching. The verse compresses a key Jain conviction: a venerated object and the means of liberation are not two things. The same wheel is at once shrine (worthy of homage), teaching (dharma-cakra) and vehicle (bohittha) across the bhava-jala-nidhi, the ocean of saṁsāra. Veneration, instruction and deliverance collapse into one image.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Chanting this verse, the reciter pictures the radiant wheel and renews a single resolve: to take the Jina's dharma itself as the boat across saṁsāra. The pilgrimage is interior — bowing to the Dharma-cakra here is committing to let the teaching, not worldly props, carry one over.

crossReferences.text: Bāhubali's kāyotsarga and the Takṣaśilā episode of Ṛṣabha's life

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit Dictionary — 'parimaṇḍala'
sources.consultedFor: Confirming parimaṇḍala = 'circular, round, circumference' and pravara = 'best, most excellent'

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit Dictionary — 'bohittha' / 'pota' (ship)
sources.consultedFor: Verifying bohittha as 'ship, ocean-going vessel' and the jala-nidhi = ocean compound

sources.title: Bahubali (Wikipedia)
sources.consultedFor: Bāhubali's standing kāyotsarga and the Adipurāṇa source behind the Dharma-cakra/Takṣaśilā tradition

sources.title: Taxila / Takshashila (Wikipedia)
sources.consultedFor: Identifying Takṣaśilā as the historical site behind the Dharma-cakra tīrtha named in this verse

notes: The word-by-word glosses (rayaṇa=ratna, joyaṇa=yojana, cakka=cakra, titthaṁ=tīrtha, nihi=nidhi, bohittha=ship) are secure. 'bhavajala nihi' is printed as separate tokens but reads as one compound, bhava-jala-nidhi ('ocean of worldly existence'), a variant of the preface's bhava-jaladhi. The site identification of the Dharma-cakra tīrtha as Takṣaśilā follows the preface's structureArc (vv.56–58) and the broader tradition (Ṛṣabha's stay, Bāhubali's commemorative wheel); the verse itself names only 'dhamma-cakka tīrtha', so the precise legend is contextual rather than stated. Whether the jewelled pillar+wheel describes a literal cosmographic structure or a remembered monument is left as the poet phrases it; no normalization of the verbatim Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa orthography has been applied.
